Ref: {{ref}}
Repurchase Obligation Study
ESOP Liability Projection & Funding Analysis
Company
{{companyName}}
Study Date
{{studyDate}}
Valuation Date
{{valuationDate}}
Projection Period
{{projectionPeriod}}
§ 01Purpose

This study projects {{companyName}}'s obligation to repurchase shares from departing ESOP participants over a {{projectionPeriod}} horizon. The repurchase obligation — also known as the put option — is a legal requirement under ERISA: when an employee leaves the company (retirement, termination, disability, or death), the company must buy back their vested ESOP shares at fair market value.

Proper projection and funding of this obligation is essential for:

A.DOL compliance. The Department of Labor expects ESOP companies to maintain a reasonable funding plan for repurchase obligations.
B.Financial planning. Repurchase payments can strain cash flow if not anticipated and reserved for in advance.
C.Trustee oversight. The independent ESOP trustee is responsible for ensuring the company can meet its obligations to participants.
D.Lender confidence. SBA and conventional lenders evaluate repurchase liability as part of debt service capacity.
§ 02Current ESOP Status

The following summarizes the current state of the {{companyName}} ESOP trust as of {{valuationDate}}:

{{totalShares}}
{{currentStockPrice}}
{{totalTrustValue}}
{{totalParticipants}}
{{averageAccountBalance}}
{{vestingSchedule}}
{{planInceptionDate}}
{{lastAppraisalDate}}
§ 03Assumptions

This projection is based on the following assumptions. Changes in any of these variables will materially affect the repurchase liability estimates.

{{stockAppreciationRate}}
{{turnoverRate}}
{{averageTenure}}
{{distributionTiming}}
{{ebitdaGrowthRate}}
{{diversificationElections}}
{{earlyRetirementProvisions}}
{{installmentPeriod}}
§ 04Repurchase Liability Projection

The table below projects estimated annual distributions and cumulative repurchase liability over {{projectionPeriod}}:

Year Est. Distributions Cumulative Liability Stock Price Funding Gap / Surplus
Year 1{{year1Distributions}}{{year1Cumulative}}{{year1StockPrice}}{{year1Funding}}
Year 2{{year2Distributions}}{{year2Cumulative}}{{year2StockPrice}}{{year2Funding}}
Year 3{{year3Distributions}}{{year3Cumulative}}{{year3StockPrice}}{{year3Funding}}
Year 4{{year4Distributions}}{{year4Cumulative}}{{year4StockPrice}}{{year4Funding}}
Year 5{{year5Distributions}}{{year5Cumulative}}{{year5StockPrice}}{{year5Funding}}
Year 6{{year6Distributions}}{{year6Cumulative}}{{year6StockPrice}}{{year6Funding}}
Year 7{{year7Distributions}}{{year7Cumulative}}{{year7StockPrice}}{{year7Funding}}
Year 8{{year8Distributions}}{{year8Cumulative}}{{year8StockPrice}}{{year8Funding}}
Year 9{{year9Distributions}}{{year9Cumulative}}{{year9StockPrice}}{{year9Funding}}
Year 10{{year10Distributions}}{{year10Cumulative}}{{year10StockPrice}}{{year10Funding}}
Total{{totalDistributions}}{{totalCumulative}}{{totalFundingGap}}
Note: Actual distributions will vary based on employee departures, stock price changes, and distribution elections. This model should be updated annually with current data.
§ 05Funding Strategy

The following funding sources are available to meet the projected repurchase obligation:

A.Internal cash flow. Annual contributions from operating cash flow to a repurchase reserve account. Target: {{annualReserveContribution}} per year.
B.SBA loan capacity. Remaining borrowing capacity under the existing SBA 7(a) facility. Available: {{sbaAvailableCapacity}}.
C.Repurchase reserve account. Current balance: {{reserveAccountBalance}}. Earnings rate: {{reserveEarningsRate}}.
D.Key man life insurance. Policy face value: {{insuranceFaceValue}}. Beneficiary: ESOP trust. Covers: {{insuranceCoverage}}.
E.External credit facility. Committed line of credit for repurchase funding. Amount: {{creditFacilityAmount}}.
F.Section 1042 reinvestment. Owner's 1042 rollover proceeds available for reinvestment. Amount: {{section1042Amount}}.
Total funding capacity: {{totalFundingCapacity}} — {{fundingCoverageStatus}}
§ 06Risk Factors

The following scenarios could cause actual repurchase liability to exceed these projections:

A.Accelerated turnover. If employee turnover exceeds {{turnoverRate}} — due to layoffs, voluntary departures, or management changes — repurchase obligations will increase earlier than projected.
B.Stock price appreciation above forecast. If the company outperforms the {{stockAppreciationRate}} growth assumption, each departing participant's shares will be worth more, increasing the per-distribution cost.
C.Mass retirements. A cohort of long-tenured employees retiring within a short window (e.g., Baby Boomer wave) could create a concentrated liability spike.
D.Diversification elections. Participants age 55+ with 10+ years of service may elect to diversify up to 25% (50% in the sixth year) of their account balance, requiring accelerated distributions.
E.Early retirement provisions. If the plan permits distributions before normal retirement age, participants may trigger repurchase obligations sooner than the standard schedule assumes.
§ 07Recommendations

Based on the projections and risk analysis above, the following actions are recommended:

1.Establish annual reserve contributions. Fund the repurchase reserve account with {{annualReserveContribution}} per year from operating cash flow to build a liquidity buffer.
2.Maintain key man insurance. Ensure life insurance policies naming the ESOP trust as beneficiary remain current and adequate.
3.Secure standby credit facility. Establish or renew a committed line of credit specifically designated for repurchase obligation funding.
4.Update annually. This study should be refreshed each year following the annual FMV appraisal to reflect current stock price, participant demographics, and cash flow projections.
5.Monitor diversification elections. Track participants approaching age 55 with 10+ years of service to anticipate diversification-driven distributions.
Next review date: {{nextReviewDate}} — following the annual FMV appraisal cycle.
—Forhemit Transition Stewardship
Forhemit Stewardship Management Co.
California Public Benefit Corporation
